notes: Per state: 4 standard clues (clues[], hardest->easiest), 1 bonusClue (hardest tier, for expert mode), plus capital, region (for regional rounds), bird, flower, and a didYouKnow fun fact shown after a correct guess. State flag image files should be sourced separately by filename convention e.g. flags/{state-slug}.svg

clues: Rosa Parks refused to give up her bus seat here in 1955, sparking the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
Home to the U.S. Space & Rocket Center, where Saturn V rockets were tested.
Nicknamed 'The Yellowhammer State' after its state bird.
Its capital, Montgomery, was once called the 'Cradle of the Confederacy.'
states.bonusClue: Home to the first-ever 911 emergency call, placed in Haleyville in 1968.
states.didYouKnow: Mardi Gras celebrations actually began in Mobile, Alabama, before New Orleans.

clues: Purchased from Russia in 1867 for about two cents an acre, mocked at the time as 'Seward's Folly.'
Home to Denali, the tallest peak in North America.
The only U.S. state with a coastline on the Arctic Ocean.
By far the largest U.S. state by land area, despite one of the smallest populations.
states.bonusClue: Contains more coastline than all other U.S. states combined.
states.didYouKnow: Alaska has far more caribou than people.

clues: The last of the contiguous 48 states admitted to the Union, in 1912.
One of the few states that doesn't observe daylight saving time.
Home to the Grand Canyon.
Nicknamed the Grand Canyon State.
states.bonusClue: The original London Bridge was dismantled and rebuilt here, in Lake Havasu City.
states.didYouKnow: Thanks to a 19th-century Army experiment with camels, it's technically illegal to hunt camels in Arizona.

clues: Home to the only spot in the U.S. where the public can dig for diamonds in their original volcanic source.
Bentonville here is the headquarters of Walmart, the world's largest retailer.
Former President Bill Clinton served as governor here.
Nicknamed the Natural State.
states.bonusClue: The very first Walmart store opened here, in Rogers, in 1962.
states.didYouKnow: Stuttgart, Arkansas hosts the World Championship Duck Calling Contest every year.

clues: Gold discovered at Sutter's Mill here in 1848 sparked a famous rush of settlers.
Home to the coast redwoods, the tallest trees on Earth.
The most populous state in the country.
Nicknamed the Golden State.
states.bonusClue: Home to Badwater Basin in Death Valley, the lowest point in North America.
states.didYouKnow: California grows the vast majority of the almonds sold in the U.S.

clues: The only U.S. state entirely above 1,000 meters in elevation.
Its borders are drawn almost entirely from straight lines of latitude and longitude.
Pike's Peak here inspired the song 'America the Beautiful.'
Nicknamed the Centennial State, admitted in 1876.
states.bonusClue: Mesa Verde here preserves centuries-old Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellings.
states.didYouKnow: Colorado has more mountains over 14,000 feet, called 'fourteeners,' than any other state.

clues: Home to the Hartford Courant, the oldest continuously published newspaper in the U.S.
Sometimes called the 'Insurance Capital of the World' for its concentration of insurers.
One of the original 13 colonies.
Nicknamed the Constitution State.
states.bonusClue: New Haven is home to Louis' Lunch, which claims to have served the first hamburger in America.
states.didYouKnow: The first American telephone directory was published in New Haven in 1878.

clues: The first state to ratify the U.S. Constitution, on December 7, 1787.
Has no state sales tax, drawing shoppers from neighboring states.
Home to more incorporated businesses than actual residents.
Nicknamed the First State.
states.bonusClue: Named after Thomas West, Baron De La Warr, an early colonial governor of Virginia.
states.didYouKnow: Delaware charges no sales tax at the state, county, or city level.

clues: Home to the only living coral barrier reef in the continental U.S.
Spanish explorer Ponce de León came here searching for the mythical Fountain of Youth.
NASA launches its crewed missions from Kennedy Space Center here.
Nicknamed the Sunshine State.
states.bonusClue: St. Augustine here is the oldest continuously occupied European-founded city in the U.S., dating to 1565.
states.didYouKnow: Florida is the only place on Earth where alligators and crocodiles live side by side in the wild.

clues: The last of the original 13 colonies to be founded, in 1733.
Atlanta's airport is the busiest in the world by passenger traffic.
World headquarters of Coca-Cola.
Nicknamed the Peach State.
states.bonusClue: The nation's top producer of peanuts.
states.didYouKnow: The Girl Scouts of the USA were founded in Savannah, Georgia, in 1912.

clues: The only U.S. state made up entirely of islands, formed by volcanic activity.
The only state that grows coffee commercially.
The last state admitted to the Union, in 1959.
Nicknamed the Aloha State.
states.bonusClue: Home to Iolani Palace, the only royal palace on U.S. soil.
states.didYouKnow: Hawaii is the only U.S. state that grows cacao beans for chocolate.

clues: Home to Hells Canyon, deeper than the Grand Canyon.
Produces roughly a third of the nation's potatoes.
Nicknamed the Gem State.
Its capital is Boise.
states.bonusClue: Home to Sun Valley, site of the world's first chairlift ski resort, in 1936.
states.didYouKnow: Idaho has more miles of white-water rivers than any other state.

clues: Chicago is home to the world's first skyscraper, built in 1885.
Route 66, the 'Mother Road,' begins in Chicago.
Nicknamed 'Land of Lincoln' for the president who launched his career here.
Once home to the Sears (now Willis) Tower, briefly the world's tallest building.
states.bonusClue: The original 1955 Des Plaines McDonald's still stands here, preserved as a museum.
states.didYouKnow: The first Ferris wheel was built for the 1893 World's Fair in Chicago.

clues: Its limestone quarries supplied stone for the Empire State Building.
Nicknamed the Hoosier State, a term whose exact origin is still debated.
Hosts the Indianapolis 500, the largest single-day sporting event in the world.
One of several states carved out of the old Northwest Territory.
states.bonusClue: Indianapolis is home to the world's largest children's museum.
states.didYouKnow: The town of Alexandria, Indiana is home to the world's largest ball of paint.

clues: Nearly all of the state's land is devoted to farming.
Kicks off every presidential election cycle by holding the nation's first caucuses.
Produces more corn than any other state.
Nicknamed the Hawkeye State.
states.bonusClue: The only state whose eastern and western borders are formed entirely by rivers, the Mississippi and Missouri.
states.didYouKnow: Iowa has produced some of the world's largest popcorn balls at state fairs.

clues: The geographic center of the contiguous United States lies near here.
The fictional setting Dorothy leaves behind in 'The Wizard of Oz.'
One of the country's leading wheat producers.
Nicknamed the Sunflower State.
states.bonusClue: Home to the world's largest ball of twine, in Cawker City.
states.didYouKnow: Kansas grows enough wheat each year to bake billions of loaves of bread.

clues: Home to Mammoth Cave, the longest known cave system on Earth.
The birthplace of bourbon whiskey.
Hosts the Kentucky Derby every year.
Nicknamed the Bluegrass State.
states.bonusClue: Fort Knox here holds a large share of the United States' gold reserves.
states.didYouKnow: Some Kentucky counties have more registered horses than people.

clues: Uses 'parishes' instead of counties for its local government divisions.
The 1803 purchase named after this state doubled the size of the young United States.
New Orleans is famous for its Mardi Gras celebrations.
Nicknamed the Pelican State.
states.bonusClue: Much of New Orleans sits below sea level.
states.didYouKnow: Louisiana is the only state with an official state meal, gumbo.

clues: The easternmost state, first in the continental U.S. to see the sunrise.
Produces about 90 percent of the nation's lobster.
The only U.S. state name with just one syllable.
Nicknamed the Pine Tree State.
states.bonusClue: Its coastline includes more than 4,600 islands.
states.didYouKnow: Maine grows more wild blueberries than any other state.

clues: Francis Scott Key wrote 'The Star-Spangled Banner' after watching the bombardment of Fort McHenry here.
Home to the headquarters of both the NSA and NIH.
Baltimore's harbor is one of the oldest continuously operating in the U.S.
Nicknamed the Old Line State.
states.bonusClue: Home to the U.S. Naval Academy, in Annapolis.
states.didYouKnow: Maryland's official state sport is jousting.

clues: Site of the first Thanksgiving, near Plymouth.
Home to Harvard, the oldest institution of higher learning in the U.S.
The Boston Marathon is the world's oldest annual marathon.
Nicknamed the Bay State.
states.bonusClue: Boston Common, founded in 1634, is the oldest public park in America.
states.didYouKnow: Basketball was invented in Springfield, Massachusetts, in 1891.

clues: The only U.S. state made up of two separate peninsulas.
Borders four of the five Great Lakes.
Detroit is considered the birthplace of the American auto industry.
Nicknamed the Wolverine State.
states.bonusClue: Mackinac Island here has banned almost all motor vehicles since the 1890s.
states.didYouKnow: Michigan has more shoreline than any state except Alaska.

clues: Home to Hormel Foods, which invented Spam here in the 1930s.
Home to the Mall of America, one of the largest shopping malls in the country.
Nicknamed the 'Land of 10,000 Lakes,' though it actually has over 11,000.
The Twin Cities of Minneapolis and St. Paul anchor this state.
states.bonusClue: Home to Lake Itasca, the official source of the Mississippi River.
states.didYouKnow: America's first fully enclosed shopping mall, Southdale Center, opened in Minnesota in 1956.

clues: Widely considered the birthplace of the blues music genre.
Named for the river running through it, from an Ojibwe phrase meaning 'great river.'
The last state to formally ratify the 13th Amendment abolishing slavery, doing so symbolically in 1995.
Nicknamed the Magnolia State.
states.bonusClue: Coca-Cola was first bottled here, in Vicksburg, in 1894.
states.didYouKnow: The teddy bear is named after an incident involving Theodore Roosevelt on a Mississippi hunting trip.

clues: The ice cream cone is said to have been invented here at the 1904 World's Fair.
Birthplace of both Mark Twain and Harry Truman.
The Gateway Arch in St. Louis marks this state's role in westward expansion.
Nicknamed the 'Show Me State.'
states.bonusClue: Home to more than 6,000 recorded caves, earning it the nickname 'The Cave State.'
states.didYouKnow: Missouri has more than 6,000 recorded caves, more than almost any other state.

clues: Once a major copper mining hub, especially around the city of Butte.
The fourth-largest state by area, yet one of the least densely populated.
Home to Glacier National Park.
Nicknamed Big Sky Country.
states.bonusClue: Home to the Berkeley Pit, a former copper mine now filled with striking blue-green toxic water.
states.didYouKnow: Montana is home to more species of mammals than almost any other state.

clues: Kool-Aid was invented here, in the town of Hastings.
Home to the only unicameral, or one-house, state legislature in the country.
Chimney Rock here was a famous landmark along the Oregon Trail.
Nicknamed the Cornhusker State.
states.bonusClue: Arbor Day, the tree-planting holiday, was founded here in 1872.
states.didYouKnow: Omaha is home to the Lied Jungle, one of the largest indoor rainforests in the world.

clues: The driest state in the country, mostly covered by desert.
Legalized gambling statewide back in 1931.
Home to Las Vegas, the entertainment capital of the world.
Nicknamed the Silver State.
states.bonusClue: The secretive Area 51 military site sits within its borders.
states.didYouKnow: Nevada is both the driest and one of the most mountainous states in the country.

clues: Home to Mount Washington, famous for some of the most extreme weather on Earth.
Traditionally holds the first primary of every presidential election.
Its state motto is 'Live Free or Die.'
Nicknamed the Granite State.
states.bonusClue: Its famous Old Man of the Mountain rock formation collapsed in 2003.
states.didYouKnow: New Hampshire has no general sales tax and no tax on wage income.

clues: Thomas Edison ran his famous research lab in Menlo Park here.
Credited with the first drive-in movie theater and the modern boardwalk.
The most densely populated state in the country.
Nicknamed the Garden State.
states.bonusClue: Hoboken is considered the birthplace of organized baseball, hosting the first recorded game in 1846.
states.didYouKnow: New Jersey has more diners than any other state, earning it the nickname 'Diner Capital of the World.'

clues: Site of the first atomic bomb test, the Trinity test, in 1945.
Home to Roswell, forever tied to a famous 1947 UFO incident.
Its flag features the red Zia sun symbol.
Nicknamed the Land of Enchantment.
states.bonusClue: Home to Carlsbad Caverns, one of the largest and deepest cave systems in the world.
states.didYouKnow: New Mexico produces more chile peppers than any other state.

clues: The Erie Canal helped turn one of its cities into a major shipping port.
Niagara Falls straddles this state's border with Canada.
Home to the Statue of Liberty.
Nicknamed the Empire State.
states.bonusClue: Home to Lombardi's, widely credited as the first licensed pizzeria in the U.S., opened in 1905.
states.didYouKnow: The first Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade marched through New York City in 1924.

clues: Home to the Biltmore Estate, the largest privately owned home in America.
Site of the Wright Brothers' first powered flight at Kitty Hawk.
A leading U.S. producer of sweet potatoes.
Nicknamed the Tar Heel State.
states.bonusClue: Virginia Dare, the first English child born in the Americas, was born on Roanoke Island here.
states.didYouKnow: Pepsi-Cola was invented in New Bern, North Carolina.

clues: One of the least-visited states in the country.
A leading producer of sunflowers and durum wheat.
Home to Theodore Roosevelt National Park.
Nicknamed the Peace Garden State.
states.bonusClue: Near the town of Rugby lies the geographic center of North America.
states.didYouKnow: North Dakota produces more honey than any other state.

clues: More U.S. astronauts were born here than in any other state, including Neil Armstrong and John Glenn.
Birthplace of aviation pioneers Orville and Wilbur Wright.
Home to the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame in Cleveland.
Nicknamed the Buckeye State.
states.bonusClue: Cleveland installed the first electric traffic light in the U.S., in 1914.
states.didYouKnow: The first professional baseball team, the Cincinnati Red Stockings, was founded in Ohio.

clues: Site of the largest land run in U.S. history, in 1889.
Has more man-made lakes than any other state.
Credited with the invention of the shopping cart.
Nicknamed the Sooner State.
states.bonusClue: Oklahoma City installed the world's first parking meter, in 1935.
states.didYouKnow: Oklahoma has more surface water from man-made lakes than any other state at a similar latitude.

clues: Home to Crater Lake, the deepest lake in the United States.
One of the few states with no general sales tax.
Portland here is nicknamed the 'City of Roses.'
Nicknamed the Beaver State.
states.bonusClue: Portland is home to Powell's Books, one of the largest independent bookstores on Earth.
states.didYouKnow: The Oregon Dunes are tall enough that visitors sled down them like snow hills.

clues: Home to the Liberty Bell in Philadelphia.
Both the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution were signed here.
Hershey here is nicknamed 'Chocolate Town USA.'
Nicknamed the Keystone State.
states.bonusClue: The University of Pennsylvania built ENIAC, one of the first general-purpose electronic computers.
states.didYouKnow: The nation's first zoo opened in Philadelphia in 1874.

clues: The first American colony to formally declare independence from Britain, in 1776.
Its full official name was once the longest of any U.S. state.
The smallest U.S. state by land area.
Nicknamed the Ocean State.
states.bonusClue: Newport is home to the Touro Synagogue, the oldest surviving synagogue building in the U.S.
states.didYouKnow: Bristol, Rhode Island hosts the oldest continuous Fourth of July parade in the country.

clues: The first state to secede from the Union, in 1860.
The first shots of the Civil War were fired at Fort Sumter here.
A leading East Coast producer of peaches.
Nicknamed the Palmetto State.
states.bonusClue: The Myrtle Beach area has one of the highest concentrations of golf courses in the country.
states.didYouKnow: South Carolina is often credited as the birthplace of sweet tea.

clues: Site of a 19th-century gold rush in the Black Hills.
Home to the Corn Palace, a building decorated each year with corn murals.
Home to Mount Rushmore.
Nicknamed the Mount Rushmore State.
states.bonusClue: Home to the Wall Drug Store, a roadside attraction famously advertised on billboards for hundreds of miles.
states.didYouKnow: South Dakota is home to more Native American reservations than most other states.

clues: Home to Graceland, Elvis Presley's famous estate.
Birthplace of country music and home to the Grand Ole Opry.
Home to the Great Smoky Mountains, the most visited national park in the U.S.
Nicknamed the Volunteer State.
states.bonusClue: Home to one of the largest underground lakes in the world, inside Craighead Caverns.
states.didYouKnow: Nashville has a full-scale replica of the Parthenon in Athens, Greece, built in 1897.

clues: The only U.S. state that was once its own independent republic before joining the union.
'Houston' was the first word spoken from the surface of the moon, a nod to Mission Control here.
The largest state in the contiguous U.S.
Nicknamed the Lone Star State.
states.bonusClue: Pecos hosted what many consider the world's first rodeo, in 1883.
states.didYouKnow: Texas has flown under six different national flags throughout its history.

clues: Home to the Great Salt Lake, saltier than the ocean itself.
Home to five national parks, more than any other state.
Hosted the 2002 Winter Olympics.
Nicknamed the Beehive State.
states.bonusClue: Home to the Bonneville Salt Flats, a legendary site for land-speed racing records.
states.didYouKnow: Utah license plates once famously boasted 'The Greatest Snow on Earth' for its famously dry powder.

clues: The first American state or territory to abolish slavery in its constitution, in 1777.
The only New England state with no ocean coastline.
The nation's largest producer of maple syrup.
Nicknamed the Green Mountain State.
states.bonusClue: Home to the original Ben & Jerry's ice cream factory, in Waterbury.
states.didYouKnow: Vermont was its own independent republic for 14 years before joining the Union in 1791.

clues: Site of Jamestown, the first permanent English settlement in America, founded 1607.
Home to the Pentagon.
Birthplace of eight U.S. presidents, more than any other state.
Nicknamed the Old Dominion.
states.bonusClue: Home to Arlington National Cemetery.
states.didYouKnow: Virginia is nicknamed the 'Mother of Presidents' for producing more U.S. presidents than any other state.

clues: Birthplace of both Starbucks and Microsoft, in the Seattle area.
Home to Mount Rainier, one of the most heavily glaciated peaks in the lower 48.
The country's leading producer of apples.
Nicknamed the Evergreen State.
states.bonusClue: Seattle opened the world's first gas station in 1907.
states.didYouKnow: Seattle's Space Needle was built for the 1962 World's Fair.

clues: The only state formed by seceding from a Confederate state during the Civil War.
The observance of Mother's Day traces back to the town of Grafton here.
Home to the New River Gorge, one of the oldest rivers in the world.
Nicknamed the Mountain State.
states.bonusClue: The only state that lies entirely within the Appalachian Mountain region.
states.didYouKnow: The New River Gorge Bridge here was once the longest steel arch bridge in the world.

clues: Home to Taliesin, architect Frank Lloyd Wright's estate and studio.
The ice cream sundae is said to have first been served in Two Rivers here.
The nation's leading producer of cheese, nicknamed 'America's Dairyland.'
Nicknamed the Badger State.
states.bonusClue: Watertown here is home to the first kindergarten established in the U.S.
states.didYouKnow: Some Wisconsin counties have more dairy cows than people.

clues: The first U.S. territory or state to grant women the right to vote, in 1869.
The least populated state in the country.
Home to Yellowstone, the world's first national park.
Nicknamed the Equality State.
states.bonusClue: Devils Tower here became the first-ever declared U.S. National Monument, in 1906.
states.didYouKnow: Wyoming has fewer residents than any other state, and more pronghorn antelope than people.
